text: The problem is inside of my code, I need to call this method and then I need to do something with the result of that method.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: However, to get this under test, I don't want it to actually talk to the API.

text: I want to be able to run this in a hermetic environment where I can control the inputs and the outputs.

text: You can't have that in a hermetic environment that you can control.

text: So what I do in this situation, this does require a little bit more of some changing.

text: So again, let's use the same example.

text: We got that class that talks to the API.

text: Maybe there's 10 instances of where the API is called in this class, but everything is all static call.

text: So it's like API.getCustomerList.

text: You can't provide a new instance of an API.

text: Now I get it.

text: There are mock frameworks that allow you to do this.

text: If you want to use that, that's fine.

text: Use that.

text: However, if I'm trying to really make this some good quality code, I'm probably going to introduce some type of interface again.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So what I'll do is I'll look at that API class, which is static calls.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And then what I'll do is I'll create an interface that has all of the exact same calls with the exact same parameters.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So maybe there's 10 calls on it, get customers, blah, blah, blah.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And I will create an interface that has all those same calls.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Then what I will do is I'll create an implementation of that interface.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And I call this, this is what I call the static gateway pattern.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Now, I don't know if...
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Don't know where I got that name from.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: If I heard from someone else, I'm not even sure.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: But it's something I've been using for over a decade to help abstract away static calls.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And there might even be a better name for it.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I don't know.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: That's the one I've used.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It's just a static gateway pattern.

text: because you want to get rid of these static calls and so i've created an interface has the same exact method names same parameters as everything that happens in the static you know the api class it's all static i create an implementation of the interface so it might be you know my stat my api static gateway
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: implementation and again i'm just using random terms and inside of that actual implementation each one of those methods has to be implemented of course what i do inside of there is i just call the static call i just say api dot get customers api dot you know update customer api dot whatever i'm just going to use the exact static calls but what this allows me to do is i now have this interface so i'm still going to use the same exact calls i was doing before
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: But I'm introducing it, basically taking this API and creating a shim on, I call it a shim or seam on top of it.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And I'm wrapping it.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It's also known as a wrapper pattern, actually.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So I'm wrapping the API, which is a static API.

text: Then inside of my legacy class, instead of actually using the implementation that requires me to have a static calls.

text: I'll create an overloaded constructor.

text: And in this constructor, what I will do is I'll use dependency injection and it will provide the interface as a constructor parameter.

text: And then what I'll do is I'll set that equal to the local field variable for this static, you know, API.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And this is going to be my interface and say, you know, API interface.

text: equals whatever is sent in through the constructor.

text: Then I'll have perhaps another overload, which is just a regular constructor, of course.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And inside of there, what that regular constructor does with no parameters, perhaps this class has no parameters for a constructor, I'll have a default constructor.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And inside of that constructor, I'll say I'll call the overloaded constructor that provides the interface.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And in that one, I'll provide a brand new instance of the static gateway implementation.

text: So use the regular API.

text: So if anyone news up this class, like they've been using it for the last five years, it'll still work just fine.

text: However, if I want to use this class in something else, I can now provide my own mock implementation through an interface.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Again, being able to mock out all the calls that I want.

text: So it will take me a little bit of rework to do this.

text: But I am not going to have to walk down this entire object graph and change everything.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I'm basically going to wrap this.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I call it static gateway pattern.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I'm going to wrap this static call in its own interface in class.

text: And I'm going to isolate it really.

text: It's still going to do its exact same job, but it gives me this seam in the middle.

text: And this is going to give me the flexibility for anyone who wants to consume that class.

text: I can just use this implementation, the static implementation as wrapper class, and it will give me the same exact result, but allows me to control the test environment much easier.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And then if anybody just news up the instance of that old static class still, it's going to call the default constructor, which will then provide the implementation of the static gateway implementation, which is just the static calls itself anyway.

text: Those are literally the two methods that I use to introduce seams all throughout applications.
speaker: SPEAKER_00
